description: Golden dataset for the Fraction Fruit Lab tutor (/api/tutor). Each case is a (qId, prompt, studentAnswer, attemptNumber, mode) tuple the production endpoint sees, along with hand-written notes about what makes a good Pip response for that case. The harness applies rule-based checks (always) and an optional Claude-as-judge layer (when JUDGE=1). v2: rebuilt for the fruit-themed lesson (apple → watermelon → banana → orange).

input.prompt: Apple check 🍎 — which is bigger: 1/2 of the apple or 2/4 of the apple?

cases.notes: Classic misconception — kid thinks bigger numerator means bigger fraction. Pip should reference the apple-squish they just watched. Do NOT say 'same' verbatim.

input.prompt: Apple check 🍎 — which is bigger: 1/2 of the apple or 2/4 of the apple?

cases.notes: Less common but possible — opposite of the first misconception. Same apple-evidence redirect applies.

input.prompt: Apple check 🍎 — which is bigger: 1/2 of the apple or 2/4 of the apple?

cases.notes: Second wrong attempt — Pip should rephrase the apple comparison in different words. Must still be a question. Must NOT just re-read the original prompt verbatim.

input.prompt: Nice. Still with apples 🍎 — how many quarter pieces fit inside one half of the apple?

cases.notes: Kid undercounted. Pip should point them to the SQUISH they did — two apple pieces snapped into one half.

input.prompt: Nice. Still with apples 🍎 — how many quarter pieces fit inside one half of the apple?

cases.notes: Kid is thinking of the whole apple, not the half. Pip can point at the reference bar — the red section is the HALF.

input.prompt: Nice. Still with apples 🍎 — how many quarter pieces fit inside one half of the apple?

cases.notes: Rephrase the apple count question gently. Stay a question.

input.prompt: New fruit time! 🍉 Look at a watermelon cut into 6 wedges. Is 3/6 of the watermelon the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Kid hasn't transferred from apple to watermelon yet. Pip should connect it back to the apple-squish pattern: same trick, new fruit.

input.prompt: New fruit time! 🍉 Look at a watermelon cut into 6 wedges. Is 3/6 of the watermelon the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Kid is unsure on the new fruit — Pip's hint should give confidence by pointing back to the watermelon's wedges, not by telling them the answer.

input.prompt: Still watermelon 🍉 — how many of those one-sixth wedges fit inside half a watermelon?

cases.notes: Kid is reusing the '2' answer from Q2 (apple quarters). Pip should redirect — sixth-wedges are smaller, so MORE fit in a half.

input.prompt: Still watermelon 🍉 — how many of those one-sixth wedges fit inside half a watermelon?

cases.notes: Kid is counting the WHOLE watermelon, not the half. Pip should point at the half versus the whole.

input.prompt: Still watermelon 🍉 — how many of those one-sixth wedges fit inside half a watermelon?

cases.notes: Third attempt — Pip should rephrase even more gently. Don't give the answer.

input.prompt: Banana time 🍌 — cut one into 3 chunks. Is 1/3 of the banana the same as 2/6?

cases.notes: Pip should connect this to the apple-equivalence trick — same pattern, different starting fraction, different fruit.

input.prompt: Banana time 🍌 — cut one into 3 chunks. Is 1/3 of the banana the same as 2/6?

cases.notes: Tentative — Pip should validate the uncertainty and nudge toward the pattern via the banana cuts.

input.prompt: Careful 🍎🍌 — is 1/2 of an apple the same fraction-size as 1/3 of a banana?

cases.notes: CRITICAL: Kid has over-generalized the equivalence pattern across fruits. Pip must NOT affirm — must redirect to size comparison. The hint should reference how splitting into 3 makes SMALLER slices than splitting into 2.

input.prompt: Careful 🍎🍌 — is 1/2 of an apple the same fraction-size as 1/3 of a banana?

cases.notes: Kid is unsure — Pip should ask them to compare piece SIZES (1/2 slice vs 1/3 slice), not the fruits themselves.

input.prompt: Careful 🍎🍌 — is 1/2 of an apple the same fraction-size as 1/3 of a banana?

cases.notes: Rephrase the discrimination question. Must keep the kid skeptical without leading them to the wrong answer.

input.prompt: Last one! 🍊 An orange has 8 natural segments. Is 4/8 of the orange the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Kid sees 4 and 8 and thinks bigger numbers = bigger fraction. Pip should connect to the pattern: half of 8 segments is 4.

input.prompt: Last one! 🍊 An orange has 8 natural segments. Is 4/8 of the orange the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Opposite misconception — kid is sticking with the small-fraction-name. Same redirect needed.

input.prompt: Last one! 🍊 An orange has 8 natural segments. Is 4/8 of the orange the same as 1/2?

cases.notes: Final question, second attempt. Rephrase with the equivalence pattern (and orange-segment imagery) in mind.

input.prompt: Apple check 🍎 — which is bigger: 1/2 of the apple or 2/4 of the apple?

cases.notes: Edge case: empty student answer (shouldn't happen in the UI but tests robustness). Pip should still produce a helpful hint.

input.prompt: Which is bigger — 1/2 of an apple or 1/4 of an apple?

cases.notes: Lesson 2. Kid thinks bigger denominator = bigger. Pip should reference the half-vs-quarter pieces they just saw: more cuts = smaller.

input.prompt: Which is bigger — 1/3 of a banana or 1/6 of a banana?

cases.notes: Lesson 2. Pip should use a sharing analogy or piece-size reasoning — never say wrong.

input.prompt: When you cut a fruit into MORE pieces, does each piece get bigger or smaller?

cases.notes: Lesson 2 concept question, rephrase mode. Must re-ask in different words without revealing 'smaller'.

input.prompt: What is 1/4 + 1/4?

cases.notes: Lesson 3. Classic mistake: kid added the bottoms too (4+4=8). Pip should redirect: add the tops, KEEP the bottom. Reference the squish.

input.prompt: What is 2/6 + 1/6?

cases.notes: Lesson 3. Kid added bottoms (6+6=12). Pip should point out the bottom stays the same.

input.prompt: When you add fractions with the SAME bottom number, what stays the same?

cases.notes: Lesson 3 concept, rephrase mode. Re-ask without giving away 'the bottom number'.
